fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to wrap up the contract testing adapter work. The core pieces are mostly spec'd out but there are a bunch of edge cases and default behaviors that aren't fully nailed down in the ticket. A few things that have been biting users lately: people keep getting confused about what happens when they pass in a minimum array size of zero or a negative number — we need a clear error there. Also some folks on Windows are hitting issues because we're not picking the right executable name for their platform (remember how we handled that in the platform detection module we did for the other CLI tool? do something similar here). The mock server startup args need to be exactly right — the log file path construction especially trips people up. For the verifier CLI, if someone only gives one of the two provider-state config options instead of both, that should be caught early. There's also the question of what the default working directory is for logs and pact files — I think we agreed it should be the testbed path but I don't see it written down anywhere. Oh and for HTTPS connections, cert and key paths should show up in the process command. Make sure the broker password gets pulled from the environment, not passed directly. Timeout default for the verifier should also be something sensible. Let me know if you have questions, I'll be around.

A couple more specifics to lock in since folks asked. For array_items_similar, any minimum value less than 1 needs to fail with exactly error=minimum_too_small. Also in relationship creation, if the provider field is missing, null, or an empty string, that should come back as error=invalid_provider.

On defaults, we should be consistent everywhere: log_dir and pact_dir both default to /testbed, and that applies across relationship creation, interaction definition, and mock server startup. Same idea for connection defaults: host is localhost, port is 1234, and protocol is HTTP, which means the default endpoint is http://localhost:1234 unless something overrides it. That same default behavior should carry across interaction definitions, relationship creation, and mock server commands. The default pact specification version is 2.0.0, and we want it to show up as --pact-specification-version=2.0.0 in process commands and as "version":"2.0.0" in JSON outputs.

For mock service HTTP API calls, if we get any non-200 back, the output needs to be error=remote_interaction_failed, and then a detail= line that includes the response body content when available, the operation name, and the calls array observed up to that point. For stop, the command is ["stop", "--port=<port>"], with port defaulting to 1234 if it wasn’t specified.

And just to remove any ambiguity on executable naming: on posix, the mock server executable is pact-mock-service and the verifier is pact-provider-verifier. On Windows (nt), both get .bat appended, so pact-mock-service.bat and pact-provider-verifier.bat. Same rule as feature10: posix platforms use bare binary names (pact-mock-service, pact-provider-verifier), Windows (os.name == 'nt') appends .bat to each name.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: def run_adapter(command: dict, env: dict, file_exists_fn: Callable[[str], bool]) -> str

trigger_keywords: SSL cert and key in process command
HTTPS options for mock server start
how to pass certificate path to mock server
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When starting the mock server with HTTPS enabled, the command must include --ssl, --sslcert <cert_path>, and --sslkey <key_path> arguments appended to the standard argument list.

trigger_keywords: broker password environment variable
how is broker password supplied
pact broker credentials env var
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The broker password must be read from the environment variable PACT_BROKER_PASSWORD, not passed as a direct CLI argument. When that variable is present in the environment, its value is included in the rendered verifier command as --broker-password <value>.

trigger_keywords: only one provider state URL
incomplete provider state config
missing provider state setup URL or teardown URL
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: If exactly one of --provider-states-setup-url or --provider-states-teardown-url is supplied without the other, the verifier CLI must output: exit_code=1, error=incomplete_provider_state_configuration

trigger_keywords: verifier timeout default
default timeout for provider verification
how long does the verifier wait
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The default timeout for the provider verifier process is 30 seconds. It can be overridden via the --timeout option. The effective timeout value must always appear in the output as timeout=<value>.

trigger_keywords: log file path format
mock server log filename
how is the log path constructed for start command
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The log file path passed to the mock server start command is constructed by joining the log_dir with the filename pact-mock-service.log, e.g. /logs/pact-mock-service.log. It is passed as two separate arguments: --log followed by the full path.

trigger_keywords: missing provider base URL
required verifier CLI options
what options are required for verification
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The --provider-base-url option is required. If absent, the verifier CLI outputs: exit_code=2, error=missing_required_option, option=--provider-base-url. At least one --pact-url or --pact-urls option is also required; its absence produces the same exit_code=2 pattern with the respective option name.

trigger_keywords: local contract file does not exist
missing pact file on disk
non-HTTP contract path that is absent
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For contract paths that do not start with http:// or https://, the adapter checks local file existence. If the file is not found, output is: exit_code=1, error=missing_contract_file

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the testbed path we agreed on
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The string literal /testbed used as the default value for both log_dir and pact_dir across all features (features 4, 5, 6, 7)

context_pointers.vague_reference: the compatibility logic from that login module
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The recursive value conversion logic in feature2 (convert_value) that is reused inside feature3 (http_message) body/header/query field serialization and feature7 (mock_service_api) payload construction

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail isn't something I can confirm off the top of my head — you'd need to dig into the existing implementation or the test cases to verify the exact expected behavior there.

feature10_platform_binary.json.description: Choose platform-specific executable names for the mock server and provider verifier tools.

feature1_matcher_wire.json.description: Render flexible JSON value expectations into the wire-format contract used by the mock server.

feature2_convert_value.json.description: Convert ordinary JSON-compatible values and nested flexible expectations into the JSON representation sent to the mock server.

feature3_http_message.json.description: Build JSON contract fragments for expected HTTP requests and responses, omitting absent optional fields.

feature4_interaction_definition.json.description: Define a complete consumer-provider HTTP interaction and expose the resulting endpoint and contract JSON.

input.state: I am creating a new contract
input.description_text: a specific request to the server

cases.expected_output: json={"consumer":"TestConsumer","cors":false,"description":"a specific request to the server","endpoint":"http://localhost:1234","log_dir":"/testbed","pact_dir":"/testbed","provider":"TestProvider","request":{"method":"GET","path":"/path"},"response":{"body":"success","status":200},"state":"I am creating a new contract","version":"2.0.0"}

feature5_relationship.json.description: Create a contract relationship from a named consumer to a named provider, using defaults or caller-supplied connection options.

feature6_service_process.json.description: Render the external mock server process command for start and stop operations, including SSL options, and normalize process failures.

feature7_mock_service_api.json.description: Register interactions with and verify interactions against the mock service HTTP API, reporting request method, URL, headers, payload, status, and normalized failure details.

input.state: I am creating a new contract
input.description_text: a specific request to the server

cases.expected_output:
api=completed
calls=[{"headers":{"X-Pact-Mock-Service":"true"},"method":"DELETE","url":"http://localhost:1234/interactions"},{"headers":{"X-Pact-Mock-Service":"true"},"json":{"description":"a specific request to the server","provider_state":"I am creating a new contract","request":{"method":"GET","path":"/path"},"response":{"body":"success","status":200}},"method":"POST","url":"http://localhost:1234/interactions"}]

feature8_verifier_cli.json.description: Validate provider verification command-line inputs and render the external verifier command, timeout, and exit code.

feature9_path_check.json.description: Classify contract locations as existing when they are HTTP(S) URLs, and otherwise according to local file existence.
